coredata.dc:title: Introducing Schottky barrier into electrochemical response: A novel adjusting strategy for designing electrochemical sensors
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xingtao
Zhao, Minggang
Li, Hui
Song, Yawen
Chen, Shougang
coredata.dc:description: A novel strategy of using Schottky barrier as a controllable tuning factor for electrochemical detection was proposed. Our results showed that the Schottky barrier height could be tuned purposely by the charged characteristics of adsorbed molecules so that the electrochemical response could be controllably enhanced or weakened. The fabricated 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs (carbon quantum dots) foam with Schottky interfaces was successfully employed for selective electrochemical detection of dopamine. It is an effective approach to overcome the limit of selective detection of targets with similar redox properties by using electrochemical method. Rational design of Schottky barrier is promising to develop new-type and more effective electrochemical sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heterstructure
Schottky barrier
Tuning factor
Electrochemical detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The energy band diagram at the interface of CQDs-ZnO contact, where Schottky barrier is formed. (a) Schottky barrier with absorbed positively charged molecules. (b) Schottky barrier with absorbed electrically neutral molecules. (c) Schottky barrier with absorbed negatively charged molecules.
(a) Schematic diagram of the 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam. (b) The energy band diagram of Schottky interfaces.
(a–c) SEM images of the prepared 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam. (d) TEM image of the prepared CQDs. (e–f) TEM images of the CQDs decorated ZnO nanorod. (g–i) HRTEM images of the CQDs decorated ZnO nanorod.
Under the existence of DA, CV curves of the Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam (a–c) and Ni/ZnO foam (d–f) in the presence and absence of 0.5mg/ml Hb in 0.1M PBS with pH=6.5, pH=7.4 and pH=8.0, respectively.

(a) Typical i-t curve of the 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam on the successive addition of DA under stirring at 0.4V. (b) Calibration curve of DA concentration versus current. (c) Amperometric response to the addition of 10μM different analytes in the sequence of DA, UA and AA in 0.01M PBS (pH=7.4).
Comparison of performance of various DA biosensors.

head.ce:title: Introducing Schottky barrier into electrochemical response: A novel adjusting strategy for designing electrochemical sensors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Ocean University of China, 266100 Qingdao, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Optoelectronic Materials and Technologies Engineering Laboratory of Shandong, Physics Department, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Optoelectronic Materials and Technologies Engineering Laboratory of Shandong
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel strategy of using Schottky barrier as a controllable tuning factor for electrochemical detection was proposed. Our results showed that the Schottky barrier height could be tuned purposely by the charged characteristics of adsorbed molecules so that the electrochemical response could be controllably enhanced or weakened. The fabricated 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs (carbon quantum dots) foam with Schottky interfaces was successfully employed for selective electrochemical detection of dopamine. It is an effective approach to overcome the limit of selective detection of targets with similar redox properties by using electrochemical method. Rational design of Schottky barrier is promising to develop new-type and more effective electrochemical sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical detection has attracted significant interest because of its fast response, high sensitivity and low cost [1–3]. However, eliminating the interfering signal of interferent with similar redox characteristics has become a large challenge [4,5]. Unfortunately, the biomolecules sharing overlapped redox peaks are always coexisting in biological samples and are difficult to distinguish by electrochemical response, which limits the applications of electrochemical detection seriously [6]. For instance, the coexistence interfering substances of ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA) greatly affected the selective electrochemical detection of dopamine (DA), due to their similar oxidation potentials [7–10]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop an effective strategy to solve the problem. As is known to us, the energy barrier height can be adjusted in different conditions, consequently sensing performance can be enhanced by employing heterostructure as a lever in electron transfer [11–13]. Recently, heterostructure interface attracted tremendous attention to improve sensing performance of materials. For example, the formation of energy level matching Cu2O/ZnO heterostructure showed good performance in the detection of glutathione [14]. The Schottky interface contact at the end of a Metal-ZnO nanowire filed effect transistor (FET) device exhibited enhanced sensitivity than ZnO nanowire with ohmic contact in the detection of protein [15,16]. The originally light nonresponsive CuO and silicon nanowires turned light responsive due to the gating effect of the Schottky contact [17]. The above-mentioned works indicates that the Schottky interface plays a positive role in improving the photoelectric characteristics of materials that is crucial for sensing performance of sensors. However, the effects of Schottky interface on electrochemical response have not been studied and what role it would play in electrochemical detection has not been demonstrated so far. Therefore, it is desired to introduce Schottky interface into electrochemical sensors to develop the novel sensing mechanism.

ZnO is a versatile semiconductor with wide band gap and high exciton binding energy, which has been studied in various application including chemical sensors, photocatalyst and solar cells. ZnO nanomaterials have many advantages in biosensing such as high electron mobility, abundant morphologies, high Isoelectric Point, nontoxicity and biocompatibility [18–21]. Additionally, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a class of ‘zero dimensional’ carbon nanomaterials, have attracted considerable attention due to their unique properties. CQDs can act as outstanding materials in the design of biosensors with high chemical stabilities, high aqueous solubility, low toxicity, good electron conductivity and excellent biocompatibility [22–25]. Moreover, the Schottky potential barrier can be fabricated at the CQDs-ZnO interface because the work function of n-type ZnO is lower than that of CQDs [26]. Therefore, it is promising to fabricate substantive Schottky interfaces by employing ZnO and CQDs.

Herein, Schottky interfaces were fabricated in the 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam and the effects on electrochemical detection was studied. The results showed electrochemical response could be controllably enhanced or weakened by changing the Schottky barrier height when different charged biomolecules adsorbed at the exposed Schottky interfaces. The adjusting mechanism was further studied and the model was established. It is an effective strategy to differentiate the targets with similar redox properties and decrease the interfering signals by introducing Schottky interface in electrochemical detection. The 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam was applied for electrochemical detection of DA. Excellent selectivity and high sensitivity were achieved by using the tuning effects of Schottky barrier. Therefore, employing Schottky barrier as tuning factors is a novel strategy to develop more effective electrochemical sensors.

ZnO nanorods were synthesized according to the previous report [27]. First, a piece of Ni foam as the substrate was cleaned by sonication in acetone, alcohol and deionized water for 15min respectively, then dried with N2 flow. 0.416g Zn(NO3)2∙6H2O was dissolved in 38ml deionized water. 2ml NH3∙H2O was added drop by drop into the solution after magnetically stirring for 10min. The Ni foam was immersed into the solution and autoclave was kept at 95°C for 3h, followed by rinsing and drying at 60°C.

CQDs were synthesized by the method of Dong et al. [28]. In a typical procedure, 2g citric acid monohydrate (CA) was added into a beaker and heated at 200°C for 20min. Then, 30ml 0.05g/ml NaOH solution was poured into the obtained orange liquid under vigorous stirring, finally the aqueous solution of CQDs was obtained.

10ml obtained CQDs solution was diluted by 40ml ethanol, subsequently 1ml diluted solution was spin-coated on the 3D Ni/ZnO foam at the speed of 1500rpm for 30s. Finally the obtained sample was dried at 80°C for 12h.

The morphologies of samples were characterized using S-4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained with HITACHIH-7650. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were obtained using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation over the 2θ ranges from 2θ=5° to 80° at the scanning speed of 4°/min. Energy Dispersive Spectra (EDS) were measured with S-4800 SEM. All the electrochemical experiments were performed with a CHI600E workstation (Shanghai Chenhua) with a conventional three electrode system which consists of platinum (Pt) electrode as counter electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode as reference electrode and 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3mm in diameter) as working electrode. All electrichemical measurements were carried out in 0.01M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) at room temperature.

The XRD patterns of the 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam is shown in Fig.S1 a. The diffraction peaks corresponding to (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103) and (113) planes of ZnO were identified to the hexagonal ZnO crystalline phase with a wurtzite structure (JCPDS card No. 36-1451). The sharp peaks of Ni were also observed and no other impurities phase appeared. The EDS element mapping images confirm the coexistence of C, Ni, Zn and O element. The morphologies of the synthesized 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam were presented in Fig. 1 . We can see that the as-prepared Ni foam was covered with amounts of flowerlike ZnO (Fig. 1a). The detailed structure is observed from the higher resolution SEM images (Fig. 1b–c). The flowerlike ZnO (diameter about 500 nm∼1μm, length about 15μm) were assembled by uniform nanorods and each individual nanorod from the flower shared the same geometric center, which provided enough sites for decorating CQDs and integrated channels for electron transport. The morphologies of CQDs were observe by TEM as their sizes were too small to be examined by SEM. As shown in Fig. 1d, the CQDs with a narrow size about 3nm were spherical and monodispersed. From Fig. 1e–f, it can be observed that the CQDs distributed as islands on the surface of ZnO nanorods, which benefited for fabricating large amounts of Schottky interfaces and partially exposing ZnO. The appreciate exposure of Schottky interfaces is necessary for changing the interface electronic state. Fig. 1g–i exhibited the high resolution TEM images of CQDs decorated ZnO nanorods. As shown in Fig. 1g, the lattice space of 0.282nm agrees well with the (100) crystallographic plane in the hexagonal ZnO crystalline phase, which is consistent with the XRD result. And the (301) crystallographic plane with the lattice space of 0.257nm in the hexagonal Chaoite phase was displayed in Fig. 1i, which confirmed the existence of CQDs. In all, the 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam afforded numerous Schottky interfaces and integrated electron transport pathways.

XPS analyses were utilized to investigate the chemical state of 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam. The elements of Zn, O and C can be observed on the surface of 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam (Fig. S2). The elements of Zn and O were corresponding to ZnO, and the C element was attributed to CQDs. The peak located at binding energy of 1021.6eV and 1044.5eV were assigned to Zn 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of Zn2+ in ZnO (Fig. S2 a). In the O 1s XPS spectra of Fig. S2 b, the peak located at 531.5eV was assigned to O2− in ZnO [29]. In the C 1s XPS spectra, the peak marked at 284.8eV and 289.5eV were associated with sp2 coordinated carbon hybridization and OCOH oxygen-containing functional groups, respectively [30,31]. The results of XPS confirm that the CQDs were incorporated with ZnO.

The effect of Schottky interfaces on electrochemical response was investigated. Hemoglobin (Hb) was selected as charged molecules, and pH was adjusted to control its charged properties. It is known that Hb with an isoelectric point (pI) of 7.4 are positively charged at pH=6.5 (pI>pH), negatively charged at pH=8.0 (pI<pH) and nearly electrically neutral at pH=7.4 (pI=pH), respectively [32]. In addition, the electrochemical response of DA was taken as the reference line for measuring the effect of Schottky interface. The corresponding electrochemical response to Hb with different pH values was shown in Fig. 2 a–c. It is observed that the oxidation peak increased significantly after adding Hb at pH=6.5 (Fig. 2a). However, only negligible change of the oxidation peak was found at pH=7.4 (Fig. 2b). When the pH value increased to 8.0, the oxidation peak drastically decreased (Fig. 2c). The compared results demonstrated that the charged properties of the adsorbed molecules have adjustable effects on the electrochemical response. In addition, the contrast experiments were further performed on the 3D Ni/ZnO foam (Fig. 2d–f) and 3D Ni/CQDs foam (Fig.S4 a-c) at various pH values. It is observed that all of them showed negligible responses to the charged Hb molecules. Similar results were also found on the pure ZnO and CQDs. The above results indicated that the adjustable effects were attributed to the Schottky interfaces fabricated between CQDs and ZnO rather than other interfaces and the intrinsic properties of materials.

The above electrochemical adjusting effects of the 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam to charged molecules can be explained from the energy band diagram of the ZnO/CQDs interface (Scheme 1 ). As shown in Fig. S3, the typical I–V curve of the ZnO/CQDs in air exhibited a nonlinear behavior implying the successful formation of Schottky barrier [33].The original Schottky barrier height (Φ) is determined by the work function difference between the CQDs and ZnO, and the interface state [16] (Scheme 1b). Φ kept in original state when electrically neutral Hb molecules were attached to the junction area at pH=7.4 and thus the conductance of the biosensor unchanged, which resulted in the negligible change of oxidation peak current (Fig. 2b). However, Φ decreased once the positively charged Hb molecules were attached to the ZnO/CQDs interfaces at pH=6.5 (Scheme 1a). This was attributed to that the adsorbed positively charged molecules would induce negative charges, which resulted in a drop of the conduction band of ZnO and depressed the Φ to a lower level [34]. In such a case, electrons overcame the lower Schottky barrier than before when they transferred from CQDs to ZnO. As a result, the conductance of the 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam increased and the oxidation peak was enhanced sharply (Fig. 2a). On the contrary, when the negatively charged Hb molecules were adsorbed at the ZnO/CQDs interfaces, Φ increased, which decreased the amount of electrons transporting across the contact interface and thus the conductance of the 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam decreased [35] (Scheme 1c). Under the circumstance, the oxidation peak dropped sharply, consistent with the data shown in Fig. 2c. The above theoretical analysis demonstrated that the electrochemical response can be controllably tuned through introducing the Schottky interface in the electron transporting channels.

Further investigation was performed to explore the above theory. The tuning mechanism responsible for electrochemical response is the energy band structure as depicted in Scheme 2 b. CQDs, Ni foam and ZnO formed a structure similar to metal−semiconductor−metal (M−S−M) and the Schottky barrier at one M−S contact is different from the other [36]. When electrons transported from CQDs to ZnO, they must overcome a Schottky barrier upping to a higher energy band, therefore the increase and decrease of Φ affected the electrons transporting significantly. However, the Schottky barrier fabricated between ZnO and Ni had no hindering effect on the electron transporting from ZnO to Ni since the conduction band of ZnO is higher than the Fermi level of Ni. As a result, the 3D Ni/ZnO foam exhibited tiny responses to the charged Hb molecules, the corresponding measurement results were shown in Fig. 2d–f. According to the above tuning theory, the increase and decrease of Φ was attributed to the change of conduction band of ZnO. In order to verity that theory, further contrast experiment was performed. As shown in Fig.S 4, the negligible responses were observed after addition of Hb molecules when ZnO were fully covered with CQDs. Under this condition, the drop of the energy band level in the ZnO region could not be effectively achieved, as a result, Φ was unchanged and no adjustable effect on the electron transport occurred. The above compared results indicate that both the direction and the appreciate exposure of Schottky interfaces are necessary for achieving the electrochemical adjusting effects.

The 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam was further applied for electrochemical detection of DA. Fig. 3 a displayed the typical current-time curve of the biosensor at 0.4V with successive addition of DA step by step under stirring. It is observed that the biosensor displayed a sensitive response to the change of DA concentration. The current showed favorable increase with addition of DA and the linear range was from 10μM to 580μM with a correction coefficient R=0.998 (Fig. 3b). This kind of biosensor showed a high sensitivity of 1310.85μAmM−1 cm−2 and low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.16μM (based on the signal-to-noise ratio of 3). The electrochemical performance of the 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam was compared with previous reports Table 1 , and higher sensitivity was found.

Due to the interference of the coexistence in biological systems, the selectivity of the biosensor was explored by adding DA, UA and AA under stirring. As shown in Fig. 3c, the current increased sharply after adding DA at 0.4V, while the neglect fluctuation of current was observed upon the addition of the same concentration of UA and AA. The result indicated that the biosensor exhibited the excellent selectivity to DA, which can be explained by the above discussed theory. DA is positively charged at pH=7.4 as its pKa (dissociation constant) is 8.87 [37], therefore Φ decreased after absorption of positively charged DA molecules, leading to the significant increase of current. In contrast, AA (pKa=4.1) and UA (pKa=3.7) are negatively charged at pH=7.4 [38], Φ thus increased once they attached to the Schottky interfaces, and the corresponding current signals were strongly weakened. Furthermore, it is found that the biosensor exhibited poor selectivity to DA when Schottky interfaces as the tuning factors were absent or fully covered (Fig.S5), indicating the excellent selectivity is attributed to the tuning effects of Schottky interfaces. The results demonstrated that the rational design and use of Schottky barrier can overcome some limits of electrochemical detection.

The stability of fabricated biosensor was investigated by successively cycling the electrode for 10 repeated cycles. It is observed that the response current was about 80% of initial current after 10 cycles (Fig. S6 (a)). The long-term stability was studied by measuring the CV response to 0.5mM DA every two days for two weeks, and 82% of the first day response remained (Fig. S6 (b)). The stability can be improved by developing approaches, but the strategy demonstrated here is rather general.

In summary, we developed a novel mechanism introducing Schottky barrier as tuning factor for electrochemical response. The key idea is tuning the Schottky barrier height to controllably enhance or weaken the electrochemical response. The method introduced here can be applied for effectively decreasing the interfering signal of coexisting interference with similar redox properties. The 3D Ni/ZnO/CQDs foam was synthesized and the Schottky barrier was utilized for selectively electrochemical detection of DA. It is a novel strategy to overcome some limit of electrochemical detection and develop more effective electrochemical sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was sponsored by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51602298, 51572249), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2016EMB02, ZR2016FB05), National High Technology Research and Development Program of China (2013A2041106), Qingdao City Programs for Science and Technology Plan Projects (15-9-1-82-jch), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University (201562011).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2017.07.151.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
